name: Chemtrails Full
description: The chemtrail conspiracy theory posits that some trails left by aircraft are not ordinary condensation trails ('contrails') but consist of chemical or biological agents deliberately sprayed at high altitudes for undisclosed purposes. Proponents of this theory claim these 'chemtrails' are part of a secret large-scale atmospheric program (SLAP) orchestrated by government agencies, military organizations, or other powerful entities. Alleged motivations range from weather modification and population control to military experimentation and geoengineering. Evidence cited includes persistent contrails that spread out and form hazy skies, unusual atmospheric phenomena, and anecdotal reports of health problems linked to aerial spraying. The theory emerged in the late 1990s and gained traction through online forums and alternative media. Mainstream science and governmental organizations, including NASA, the EPA, and numerous universities, consistently debunk the chemtrail theory. They explain that persistent contrails are a natural phenomenon caused by aircraft exhaust freezing in the cold, humid conditions of the upper atmosphere. The spreading effect is attributed to wind shear and atmospheric conditions. Chemical analyses of air and precipitation samples have consistently failed to find evidence of unusual substances or elevated levels of chemicals that would support the chemtrail claims. The alleged health effects are generally attributed to other environmental factors or misdiagnosis. The scientific consensus is that chemtrails are simply contrails and that the conspiracy theory lacks any credible scientific basis.
subtitle: Alleged chemical spraying from aircraft.

claims.claim: Aircraft are deliberately spraying chemicals into the atmosphere.
claims.evidence: Persistent contrails that spread out and form hazy skies; chemical analysis of rainwater showing elevated levels of certain elements (often debunked or misinterpreted).
claims.criticism: Persistent contrails are normal contrails under specific atmospheric conditions. Rainwater analyses have not shown consistent or significantly elevated levels of chemicals beyond normal background levels.

claims.claim: The purpose of chemtrails is weather modification.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal correlations between chemtrail sightings and unusual weather events; claims of government documents hinting at weather control programs.
claims.criticism: Weather modification is a known field of research, but there is no evidence linking it to chemtrails. Government documents often refer to legitimate research programs, not secret operations.

claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports of respiratory issues, fatigue, and other symptoms linked to chemtrail sightings.
claims.criticism: No scientific evidence links chemtrails to health problems. Reported symptoms are often attributed to pre-existing conditions, environmental factors, or mass psychogenic illness.

keyFigures.name: Patrick Minnis
keyFigures.role: Atmospheric scientist who has debunked chemtrail claims.

keyFigures.name: David Keith
keyFigures.role: Researcher in geoengineering, sometimes misinterpreted as a proponent of chemtrails.

mythologyConnections: Connections to sky gods and manipulation of weather in ancient myths.
Parallels to narratives of secret societies controlling the world.

timeline.event: Emergence of the chemtrail theory online.
Increased media coverage and spread of the theory.
Continued belief in the theory despite scientific debunking.

sources.author: National Research Council
sources.work: Climate Intervention: Reflecting Sunlight to Cool Earth
sources.title: National Research Council
